fuzzy_prd: We need a reusable toolkit for managing independently deployable software modules — the kind of self-contained extensions that get dropped into a running application without rebuilding everything from scratch. Each module declares its own version and lists the other modules it depends on, with an optional minimum/maximum version requirement per dependency. The toolkit needs to answer a few concrete questions: does a given version string satisfy a declared requirement? which of two versions is newer? can we parse a dependency coordinate string into its parts? given a whole set of modules, can we figure out a safe load order — and if not, why not? The load-order check should handle cycles, missing dependencies, and dependencies whose present version doesn't match what was asked for. We also need a general-purpose directed-graph utility that can topologically sort vertices and report how many edges point in and out of each one. Finally, we need a way to parse a module's key-value metadata file into a normalized descriptor object. Handle it similarly to how the version-comparison logic works — return a well-typed result, don't throw raw exceptions, surface error categories clearly.

trigger_keywords: what wildcard constraint accepts any version
what constraint value means accept all
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The wildcard constraint is '*'. It accepts any version string, including non-semantic strings, without parsing the version at all. A missing or empty version-support expression also defaults to '*'.

trigger_keywords: what error is reported when version is missing
what happens when version is null in version_constraint
what neutral error category for empty version
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: If version is null or empty, the output ends with 'error=version_required'. If version is present but not a well-formed semantic version, the output ends with 'error=invalid_version'. The output always first echoes 'version=<value>' and 'constraint=<value>' before the error line.

trigger_keywords: how is an optional dependency marked
what character marks a dependency as optional
what does the question mark do in a coordinate
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A '?' immediately after the identifier (before any '@') marks the dependency optional. The '?' is stripped from the identifier in output. For example 'test?@1.0' yields pluginId=test, versionSupport=1.0, optional=true.

trigger_keywords: what is the topological ordering format
how are inDegree and outDegree listed
what order are degree entries reported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: inDegree and outDegree are each reported as a comma-separated list of 'vertex:count' entries, with vertices in ascending (lexicographic) order. The topological line lists vertices in an order where every vertex appears before the vertices it points to. reverseTopological is the exact reverse of that ordering.

trigger_keywords: what default value does plugin.requires fall back to
what is output when plugin.requires is missing
what is the default requires expression
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A missing or empty 'plugin.requires' key yields requires=* (the any-version wildcard). A missing or empty 'plugin.description' key yields description= (empty string). Dependencies are rendered as 'id@versionSupport' with a trailing '?' for optional ones; empty dependencies list yields dependencies= (empty).

trigger_keywords: what format is the wrongVersion field
what does a wrong-version entry look like
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Each wrong-version entry has the form 'dependency=<id> dependent=<id> existing=<present version> required=<required expression>'. Multiple entries are joined with ' | '. When cyclic=true, sorted is empty. notFound and wrongVersion are independent of cycle detection.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: Handle it similarly to how the version-comparison logic works — return a well-typed result, don't throw raw exceptions, surface error categories clearly
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The DefaultVersionManager class in pf4j implements checkVersionConstraint and compareVersions. The Dispatcher catches Throwable from these and translates to neutral 'error=version_required' or 'error=invalid_version' lines. PluginDependency parses coordinate strings with '?' for optional and '@' for version support.

oracle_data.fallback_response: Please refer to the start.md specification. Each action (version_constraint, version_compare, parse_dependency, resolve_dependencies, graph, parse_metadata) has a defined line-oriented output contract.

feature1_version_constraint.json.description: Evaluate whether a concrete version string satisfies a version constraint expression. The constraint may be a simple comparator, a compound range joining several comparators, or a wildcard that accepts any version. The result reports the version under test, the constraint, and whether the version satisfies it. When the version string is missing or empty the evaluation reports a neutral error indicating a version is required; when the version string is present but is not a well-formed version it reports a neutral invalid-version error.

feature2_version_compare.json.description: Compare two version strings and report their relative order. The output echoes both operands and a single word describing whether the left version is greater than, less than, or equal to the right version according to semantic-version precedence.

feature3_parse_dependency.json.description: Parse a dependency coordinate string into its three components: the dependency identifier, the required version-support expression, and whether the dependency is optional. The coordinate is a single token of the form identifier, optionally followed by a question mark to mark it optional, optionally followed by an at-sign and a version-support expression. A missing or empty version-support expression defaults to the any-version wildcard; the trailing question mark (if present) marks the dependency optional and is not part of the identifier.

feature4_resolve_dependencies.json.description: Resolve a set of plugin descriptors into a dependency-ordered load plan. Each plugin supplies an identifier, an optional own version, and a comma-separated list of dependency coordinates (identifier with optional version-support expression). The resolver reports: whether the dependency graph contains a cycle; the plugins listed in an order where every dependency precedes the plugin that needs it; any required dependency identifiers that are not present among the supplied plugins; and any dependencies whose present version does not satisfy the version-support expression required by a dependent. When a cycle is present no order can be produced. Each wrong-version entry names the dependency, the dependent that required it, the version actually present, and the required version-support expression.

cases.expected_output:
cyclic=false
sorted=p2,p1
notFound=
wrongVersion=dependency=p2 dependent=p1 existing=1.4.0 required=>=1.5.0 & <1.6.0

feature5_directed_graph.json.description: Given a directed graph described by a set of vertices and a set of directed edges (each edge a from/to pair), report four derived results: a topological ordering of the vertices (every vertex appears before the vertices it points to), the reverse of that topological ordering, the in-degree of every vertex (number of incoming edges), and the out-degree of every vertex (number of outgoing edges). Degree maps are listed with one vertex:count entry per vertex in ascending vertex order.

feature6_parse_metadata.json.description: Parse a plugin's metadata key/value map into a normalized descriptor. The recognized keys are the plugin id, description, implementation class, version, provider, a comma-separated dependencies list, a version-requires expression, and a license. The descriptor echoes each field; an absent description normalizes to the empty string, an absent requires expression normalizes to the any-version wildcard, and the dependencies list is parsed into normalized coordinate tokens (identifier, an at-sign, the version-support expression which defaults to the wildcard, and a trailing question mark when optional).

cases.expected_output:
id=alpha-plugin
description=Sample plugin one
class=com.example.plugins.SamplePlugin
version=0.0.1
provider=Acme Corp
requires=>=1
license=Apache-2.0
dependencies=beta-plugin@*,gamma-plugin@~1.0

id=beta-plugin
description=
class=com.example.plugins.SamplePlugin
version=0.0.1
provider=Acme Corp
requires=*
license=
dependencies=
